title: Jacob's Ladder Folding Block Toy
summary: A classic folding-block toy built from painted wood blocks connected by ribbons in a cascading zigzag pattern.
description:
This toy's whole appeal is an optical trick: a stack of blocks that appears to tumble endlessly downward when tipped, even though it's really just folding along a repeating ribbon pattern. Getting that illusion right depends entirely on the ribbon attachment pattern, not on anything structural in the blocks themselves.

Cut every block from a **consistent 2-1/2" wide board using a stop block on the miter saw** — even a small variance in block length breaks the cascading illusion, since the blocks have to fold flush against each other at every joint.

Paint each face and edge a different color. Kids (and honestly, most adults) will spend more time flipping this to watch the colors shift than they will figuring out how it works.

tools.note: Used with a stop block for consistent block length.

steps.title: Cut the blocks
steps.body: Cut twelve blocks 3" long from the 2 1/2"-wide hobby board, which is 3/4" thick — 36" of board plus saw kerfs, well inside the material listed. Clamp a stop block to the miter saw fence and cut all twelve against it without moving it, so every block is identical: the illusion depends on the blocks folding flush against each other at every joint, and even a 1/32" variation from block to block breaks the cascade. Do not measure and mark twelve times; that is exactly how the variation creeps in. Check the last block against the first when you are done, and cut a thirteenth as a spare if the board allows.

steps.title: Round the corners and sand
steps.body: Round over all four corners and every edge of each block, and sand the faces smooth — this is a toy that gets flipped hand to hand for hours, so a sharp arris is the defect that actually matters. Keep the rounding light and consistent from block to block; taking too much off an end shortens the flat that the next block has to lie against and softens the snap of the fold. Sand the faces flat rather than rounding them, and blow or wipe the dust off before paint.

steps.title: Paint the blocks
steps.body: Paint each block a different color on each face and edge — the shifting colors as the blocks tumble are most of what makes people keep flipping it. Work in thin coats and let each dry fully; a heavy build of paint on the faces adds thickness that keeps the blocks from folding flush, and that is the one thing that can kill the illusion after the blocks were cut perfectly. Paint the faces where the ribbons will land too, so nothing shows bare once it is assembled, and let everything cure hard before the ribbons go on.

steps.title: Cut the ribbons
steps.body: Twelve blocks make eleven joints, and each joint needs ribbon long enough to run across the face of one block, wrap over the end and land on the face of the next with a little to fasten at each end. Cut one test piece against two real blocks, fold it exactly as it will sit, and only once that piece works use it as the pattern for the rest — cutting all the ribbon first to a guessed length is how you end up 1/2" short eleven times. The 2 yards listed is 72", which is about 6 1/2" per joint and enough for a single ribbon at each joint; the traditional version uses three narrow ribbons per joint and needs roughly three times that much, so buy more if that is the one you want. Seal each cut end with a quick pass of flame or a dab of clear glue so the grosgrain does not fray.

steps.title: Attach the ribbons
steps.body: Lay two blocks end to end and fasten the ribbon so it runs down the back of the first block, over the gap and onto the back of the second, then add the next block with its ribbon attached on the OPPOSITE face from the one before — that alternation down the whole stack is the entire mechanism; get it right for two joints and just repeat it nine more times. Work one joint at a time down the row and test the fold as you go rather than nailing all eleven and hoping, because a joint fastened on the wrong face stops the cascade dead at that point. Fasten with small brads short enough not to break through the 3/4" block on the far side, and keep the ribbon flat and snug, with just enough slack for the blocks to close on each other. Hold the top block and let the stack tumble to test; if it stops partway, that joint's ribbon is on the wrong face or is too tight.
